title: Saga Pattern
description: Manages data consistency across microservices using a sequence of local transactions. Each service publishes events that trigger the next step. Two approaches: Choreography (event-based) and Orchestration (central coordinator).

title: CQRS Pattern
description: Command Query Responsibility Segregation separates read and write models. Commands mutate state, queries read state. Enables independent scaling and optimization of reads and writes.
best_practices: Separate read/write data stores when beneficial
Optimize read models for specific queries
anti_patterns: Shared models for read and write

title: Event Sourcing
description: Stores all changes to application state as a sequence of events. The current state is derived by replaying events. Provides complete audit trail and enables temporal queries.
best_practices: Use event store with ordering guarantees
anti_patterns: No snapshots for long event streams

title: Circuit Breaker Pattern
description: Prevents cascading failures by stopping requests to unhealthy services. States: Closed (normal), Open (failing, reject requests), Half-Open (testing recovery).

title: Retry Pattern
description: Automatically retries failed operations with configurable strategies. Handles transient failures in distributed systems. Must be combined with idempotency.
best_practices: Add jitter to prevent thundering herd

title: Bulkhead Pattern
description: Isolates different parts of a system so that if one fails, the others continue working. Like watertight compartments in a ship.
best_practices: Isolate critical resources with separate pools

title: API Gateway Pattern
description: Single entry point for all client requests. Handles cross-cutting concerns like authentication, rate limiting, load balancing, and request routing.

title: Service Discovery Pattern
description: Mechanism for services to find and communicate with each other dynamically. Can be client-side (services query registry) or server-side (load balancer queries registry).

title: Distributed Tracing
description: Tracking requests as they flow through multiple services. Essential for debugging, performance analysis, and understanding dependencies in distributed systems.
anti_patterns: No correlation between logs and traces

title: Eventual Consistency
description: A consistency model where the system guarantees that if no new updates are made, eventually all accesses will return the last updated value.
anti_patterns: Reading immediately after writing expecting consistency

title: Leader Election
description: Algorithm for designating a single process as the coordinator for some distributed task. Prevents split-brain and ensures single-writer guarantees.

title: Outbox Pattern
description: Ensures reliable message publishing by writing events to an outbox table in the same transaction as the business operation, then publishing them asynchronously.
best_practices: Write to outbox in same transaction
Use change data capture for publishing

title: Strangler Fig Pattern
description: Incrementally migrates a legacy system by gradually replacing specific pieces of functionality with new services until the old system is completely replaced.
best_practices: Monitor both old and new systems

title: Sidecar Pattern
description: Deploys helper components alongside the main service container. Common for logging, monitoring, proxying, and security concerns without modifying the main service.
best_practices: Use service mesh for complex networking
anti_patterns: Sidecar as single point of failure

title: Back-Pressure Pattern
description: Mechanism for downstream services to signal upstream producers to slow down when they cannot keep up with the rate of incoming messages.
